title: Surfaces of Section, Anosov Reeb Flows, and the C2-Stability Conjecture for Geodesic Flows
speaker: Marco Mazzucchelli
abstract: In this talk, based on joint work with Gonzalo Contreras, I will briefly sketch the proof of the existence of global surfaces of section for the Reeb flows of closed 3-manifolds satisfying a condition à la Kupka-Smale: non-degeneracy of the closed Reeb orbits, and transversality of the stable and unstable manifolds of the hyperbolic closed Reeb orbits.

proof_stages.label: Surfaces of section and return maps
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a closed contact three-manifold with Reeb flow, defines ordinary, global, and almost global surfaces of section, introduces the asymptotic boundary, and explains that return maps reduce Reeb dynamics to area-preserving surface dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Generic global-section theorem
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing major existence results, the speaker defines the Reeb-theoretic Kupka-Smale condition and states the main theorem that every Kupka-Smale Reeb flow on a closed three-manifold admits a global surface of section, hence generically so.

proof_stages.label: ECH and Fried surgery produce almost global sections
proof_stages.summary: Embedded contact homology supplies many plain surfaces of section, and a surgery procedure on a large transverse collection yields an almost global surface of section with finite hyperbolic asymptotic boundary.

proof_stages.label: Eliminating an asymptotic-boundary component
proof_stages.summary: A Fried-surgery lemma shows that if one constructs an auxiliary surface of section meeting an asymptotic boundary component while avoiding the old boundary, then that component disappears; a second lemma constructs such an auxiliary surface when the component has transverse homoclinics in all separatrices.

proof_stages.label: Homoclinics in all separatrices under Kupka-Smale
proof_stages.summary: For any asymptotic-boundary component of a Kupka-Smale almost global section, stable and unstable manifolds have the same closure, heteroclinic arrows can be reversed under homoclinic hypotheses, and finite heteroclinic chains plus shadowing/lambda-lemma arguments give homoclinics in every separatrix, completing the upgrade to a global section.

proof_stages.label: Applications to Anosov Reeb and geodesic flows
proof_stages.summary: The global/almost-global section machinery is applied to characterize Anosov Reeb flows by uniform hyperbolicity of the closed-orbit closure plus Kupka-Smale transversality, yielding consequences for Riemannian geodesic flows and the C2 structural stability conjecture on closed surfaces.

proof_stages.label: Hyperbolic-dynamics reduction for the Anosov characterization
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the Reeb flow satisfies the hypotheses but is not Anosov, one obtains infinitely many closed orbits, applies spectral decomposition to a hyperbolic closure of periodic orbits, chooses an infinite basic set lambda, and uses Bowen-Ruelle measure-zero properties and heteroclinic-class facts.

proof_stages.label: Area-preserving rectangle contradiction
proof_stages.summary: A global surface of section supplies a heteroclinic rectangle disjoint from lambda but bounded by its stable and unstable manifolds; recurrence and area preservation give a return map of a finite-area disk, Brouwer's translation theorem gives a periodic point there, and local maximality forces it into lambda, a contradiction.

examples.name: Birkhoff curvature-sign geodesic flows
examples.note: Birkhoff's theorem for geodesic flows on closed surfaces with everywhere positive or negative curvature is cited as historical evidence that global surfaces of section occur in important dynamical classes.
examples.engagement_reason: Birkhoff’s positive/negative curvature geodesic-flow theorem is cited as historical background without working through such a flow.

examples.name: Finitely many hyperbolic periodic orbits
examples.note: The speaker illustrates the Anosov characterization by noting that a Kupka-Smale Reeb flow with only finitely many hyperbolic closed orbits would be forced to be Anosov, contradicting density of periodic orbits for Anosov flows.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the Anosov characterization to the case of finitely many hyperbolic closed orbits and derives the contradiction that an Anosov flow must have dense periodic orbits.

passes.reason: The finite-hyperbolic-periodic-orbit scenario is the adversarial case ruled out because Anosov flows have dense periodic orbits.
The finite all-hyperbolic periodic-orbit scenario is used as an enemy case that the Anosov characterization rules out.
The finite-hyperbolic-periodic-orbit scenario is a hypothetical ruled-out consequence of the theorem rather than an actual counterexample.
The finite all-hyperbolic periodic-orbit scenario is an adversarial case ruled out by the Anosov characterization and density of periodic orbits in Anosov flows.
A hypothetical Reeb flow with only finitely many hyperbolic closed orbits is used as an impossible enemy case under the Anosov characterization.

The finite-hyperbolic-periodic-orbit scenario is invoked to be ruled out, since the theorem would force an Anosov flow with dense periodic orbits.
The finite-hyperbolic-periodic-orbit case is a sparse enemy scenario ruled out by the Anosov characterization.

examples.name: Negatively curved closed surfaces
examples.note: Negatively curved surfaces show why one cannot generically force an elliptic closed geodesic on every surface, motivating the dichotomy between creating an elliptic geodesic and the original flow being Anosov.
examples.engagement_reason: Negatively curved closed surfaces are invoked as an obstruction, with known facts stated that their closed geodesics are hyperbolic and this persists under small perturbation.

passes.reason: Negatively curved closed surfaces block the naive elliptic-geodesic generalization since all their closed geodesics are hyperbolic persistently.

Negatively curved closed surfaces obstruct the naive extension of the elliptic-geodesic theorem because all their closed geodesics remain hyperbolic under small C2 perturbations.

examples.name: Finsler geodesic-flow comparison
examples.note: Newhouse's Finsler-geodesic-flow stability theorem is used as a comparison class to emphasize that the Riemannian problem lacks the needed C2 closing lemma and is handled instead by the Anosov characterization.
examples.engagement_reason: Finsler geodesic flows are discussed as a comparison with Newhouse’s theorem and the closing lemma, but no concrete Finsler flow is worked out.

examples.name: Symmetric Finsler metrics
examples.note: An audience question asks where reversible/symmetric Finsler metrics fit, and the speaker explains that the relevant closing lemma for this restricted class appears not to be written down and is plausibly open.
examples.engagement_reason: Symmetric Finsler metrics arise only in the question period as an open comparison about reversible perturbations, with no example or construction analyzed.
